fact: July 23 is exactly 204 days into the year, which means there are exactly 161 days left until New Year's Eve!

In the Northern Hemisphere, this is peak summer heat season - the sun has been baking the ground for weeks since the solstice, making July 23 one of the hottest days of the year even though the sun isn't at its highest point anymore.
Cicadas are typically screaming their loudest around July 23 in many places - male cicadas can make sounds up to 120 decibels, which is louder than a chainsaw!
July 23 falls right in the middle of Leo season, when the zodiac lion is roaring - Leos are supposedly the most dramatic and attention-loving of all the zodiac signs.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, July 23 is deep winter, and many eucalyptus trees are dropping their bark like giant snakes shedding their skin.
The sun rises and sets at almost exactly the same time as it did on July 22 and July 24 - the changes are so tiny you'd need a stopwatch to notice the difference.
July 23 is prime thunderstorm season in many parts of North America - hot air rising from sun-baked ground creates perfect conditions for massive storm clouds that can tower 40,000 feet high.
Many flowers like sunflowers and black-eyed Susans are often at their most gigantic around July 23, having spent months growing taller in the summer heat.
In northern countries like Canada and Alaska, July 23 still has incredibly long days with the sun setting super late - some places get 18 hours of daylight or more.
July 23 is exactly one month after the summer solstice, which means Earth has traveled about 1/12th of its entire orbit around the sun since the longest day of the year.
Many lakes and rivers reach their warmest temperatures around July 23 because water takes much longer to heat up than air - it's like nature's giant bathtub finally getting perfectly warm.
Mosquitoes are typically at their most annoying around July 23 in many places because they love hot, humid weather and there's been plenty of time for their larvae to grow up in stagnant water.
